%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# B.4 Cramer's Rule

> **Section Objective:** Learn a repeatable determinant-based procedure for solving 2-by-2 linear systems — and know exactly when it works.

Consider the system \(2x + y = 5\) and \(x - y = 1\). You could use substitution, but under exam pressure you want a template you can execute without improvising. Cramer's Rule is that template: it turns a simultaneous-equation problem into three determinant calculations and two divisions.

In this section you will learn how to build the main determinant \(D\) from the coefficient matrix, how to replace one column at a time to get \(D_x\) and \(D_y\), and how to divide to find each variable. You will also learn the one condition that must hold before the method is valid — because missing it is a common exam mistake.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 Cramer's Rule: build D from coefficients, replace one column at a time for each variable, then divide. The red warning box marks the only condition that can break the method.*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777212153038-5319.png)

$$D = \begin{vmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{vmatrix} = ad - bc, \quad x = \frac{D_x}{D}, \quad y = \frac{D_y}{D}$$
*\(D\) is the determinant of the coefficient matrix; each variable is found by dividing its replacement determinant — where one column has been swapped for the constants — by \(D\).*

## 1. How the Rule Works

Follow these four steps for any 2-by-2 system:

**Step 1 — Build \(D\).** Write the coefficient matrix and compute its determinant: \(D = ad - bc\).

**Step 2 — Build \(D_x\).** Replace the \(x\)-column (the first column) with the constants \(e\) and \(f\), then compute the determinant.

**Step 3 — Build \(D_y\).** Replace the \(y\)-column (the second column) with the constants, then compute the determinant.

**Step 4 — Divide.** \(x = D_x / D\) and \(y = D_y / D\).

### WORKED EXAMPLE

System: \(2x + y = 5\) and \(x - y = 1\).

\(D = (2)(-1) - (1)(1) = -3\). Replace the \(x\)-column: \(D_x = (5)(-1) - (1)(1) = -6\). Replace the \(y\)-column: \(D_y = (2)(1) - (5)(1) = -3\).

Therefore \(x = -6 / -3 = 2\) and \(y = -3 / -3 = 1\).

#### Exam Note

Students lose marks by replacing the wrong column (e.g., replacing the \(y\)-column when solving for \(x\)) or by forgetting to divide by \(D\) at the final step.

$$\begin{aligned} 2x + y &= 5 \\ x - y &= 1 \end{aligned} \quad D = \begin{vmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{vmatrix} = -3, \quad D_x = \begin{vmatrix} 5 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{vmatrix} = -6, \quad D_y = \begin{vmatrix} 2 & 5 \\ 1 & 1 \end{vmatrix} = -3$$
*The example shows the column-replacement pattern directly: the constants replace the \(x\)-column in \(D_x\) and the \(y\)-column in \(D_y\), so \(x = (-6)/(-3) = 2\) and \(y = (-3)/(-3) = 1\).*

## 2. When Cramer's Rule Works

The method produces a unique solution only when \(D \neq 0\). The formulas \(x = D_x / D\) and \(y = D_y / D\) both require \(D\) in the denominator, so if \(D = 0\) the division is undefined and the rule cannot directly give a unique answer.

> **Key fact:** If \(D = 0\), stop. Do not continue computing \(D_x\) and \(D_y\) and then attempt to divide. Instead, state clearly that Cramer's Rule does not give a unique solution in that form.

On an exam, this is a one-line conclusion — not a reason to panic or guess. The system may have no solution or infinitely many, but that determination requires additional analysis beyond what this direct formula provides.

#### Check D First

Before computing \(D_x\) and \(D_y\), look at \(D\) first.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="exam_pattern_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 Decision flowchart: always evaluate D before proceeding. A non-zero D means continue; D = 0 means the direct formula cannot give a unique solution.*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777212348294-7822.png)

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Compute \(D\) from the coefficient matrix first — if \(D = 0\), the direct method cannot give a unique solution.
- Replace the \(x\)-column with constants for \(D_x\), and the \(y\)-column with constants for \(D_y\).
- Divide each replacement determinant by \(D\) to get the variable: \(x = D_x/D\), \(y = D_y/D\).

*In the next section we will build on this method in a new setting.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-quiz-plan" data-quiz-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
